sentences.text: Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
Yes.
Obersturm Vlasook, he was in charge of all the labor camps and the death camps which were in our area, in [NON-ENGLISH].
He was also in charge, there was a camp, was called [NON-ENGLISH].
That was like a death camp.
That was the first death camp in our area.
But he was-- he-- on this particular night-- when I start telling you about the liquidation, about the liquidation of this-- our camp, he was the one who was in charge.
So, he came down with all these SS men and Gestapo.
And like I told you before, the camp was surrounded by German and Ukrainian police.
So, and 5 o'clock in the morning-- Also, I must mention one important aspect.
We had, our camp commander was a young fellow.
He was the camp commander of Sasow.
And his name was Muntak.
He was a very young fellow.
And he was a pretty good guy.
He never-- he-- he would never harm anybody as bad.
And he used to tell us, and when it started, when we heard-- the way the Germans-- here's another way.
The way the propaganda went on, the way the Germans were getting-- executing all these Jews, it was everything so silent.
Nobody knew what was going on.
We were, like, 10 kilometers away from [NON-ENGLISH].
We did not know what was going on with the Jewish community.
That's how it was.
We had no newspaper.
We had no radios.
The only news we used to get, if somebody by coincident, by sheer luck, survived, or he run away from a camp or from a ghetto, he used to tell us these thing.
So this guy, Muntak, used to tell us--
Muntak was a German.
He was the camp commander, like I said before.
He used to tell us-- in German, I'm going to say it-- [GERMAN].
Which means, the time comes, the time is nearer, where they're going to kill you all.
But I'm not going to be around to witness that.
And the fact was, he was not around in the-- by the liquidation of the camp.
Oh, definitely.
You know?
I mean, he was part of the Germans.
He was part of the SS.
He must've known.
Well, he, this is the warning he gave us.
But he couldn't do nothing.
We couldn't do nothing.
[SIGHS]

So, anyhow, so what happened in the middle of July, I think in the 15th of July, in the Jewish calendar I observed, the [NON-ENGLISH], which is 20 days in Tammuz, which is-- which coincides with July 15, or whatever.
So, they came up and they-- under the pretext that they heard that our camp is gonna make an uprise against the Germans.
In order to make sure, to be sure that this thing is not going to happen, they want to search the camp and they want to search everybody for weapons and for ammunition.
OK.
So they took us all down.
They also brought in-- we were, in that time, I would say, maybe, about 500, 600 people.
They brought some people from-- remnants, Jews who were left, who-- they had another camp, from different areas, from small towns.
There were another 300, 400 people.
And they took us down to the ground in there, to the yard.
Was a big area.
And it was surrounded with a little fence.
And they told us all to go inside the fence, sit down with our knees under, and to put our hands behind our backs.
As the-- the Germans, the SS, and the Ukrainians surrounded us, they made a circle around us.
And they said, and this guy, Vlasook, went up on top of the step, because the camp was like, two, three stories high.
He went up on a platform.
And he started to say, he says, well, he says, I'm going to make a selection.
I'm going to search everybody.
Whoever I'm going to find a weapon or ammunition, I'm going to kill them right on the spot.
OK.
So they called up the first guy.
It was a-- a friend.
It was a-- a-- a little-- a friend of mine.
His name was-- what was his name?
Chaim.
Or whatever.
I forgot.
He was-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH], I know for sure.
They called him up.
He was in my age.
Because he was the first one to sit there.
And they told him to go upstairs, to go up.
They're going to search him.
OK.
So he went up.
The minute we start to run up, we heard a shot.
So we knew there's something happening in there.
What-- OK.
They start to call another one.
They took-- they didn't call them by name or whatever.
They just told them, [GERMAN], up.
Go up.
And, as this was going on, they had already a few people shot.
My father and me-- my father was next to me.
When my father heard that they're shooting these people, he said to me, he says, listen my child.
I am going to sacrifice my life in order so you could survive, that you should be able to survive and tell this story to the world.
I start crying.
And I say, Dad-- Dad-- [SOBBING] why-- why are you doing this?
And he says, this is it.
I want you to-- you-- you should try to survive.
There should be at least one member from our family that will survive this tragedy.
My father went up.
I heard a shot.
And that was the end of this.
And in the meantime, we saw already what happened, that nobody's gonna get out alive.
So we had one Jewish policeman.
He was a camp policeman.
He was a big man.
He took a bottle of vodka and he had an SS man over the head and he killed him.
When the Germans saw this, they gave an order.
They say, [GERMAN], which means, pull back and shoot.
So they started to throw in hand grenades.
They started to shoot from machine guns.
I was hit in my leg from a shrapnel.
But it wasn't too bad.
And we start to run.
I remember, I don't know how I got up or what I did between all the corpses.
We came down to the fence.
It was all lined up with dead bodies.
I don't know how I got above-- over it-- over that fence, with all this.
It seemed like an angel was carrying me.
And I ran away.
From 800 people, in that time, we are only about 50 survived.
From the 50, maybe 10 survive.
Because us, we went-- so we start running.
You start running, let's say, to the front, machine guns.
They're shooting on you.
You turn right.
They shooting from the right side.
You turn left.
They're shooting more on.
So, our camp was also in a big, wooded area.
So when I run away, I thought I run away, maybe, I'm away, already, maybe 100 miles.
Sure enough, I fell asleep.
And I found another friend of mine who was with me.
And we got together.
And we laid down in a ditch in the forest.
And we thought, we safe, that we are a hundred miles away from the camp.
Sure enough, in the morning-- it was still dark-- we heard Germans with dogs, searching for us.
So I wake up my friend.
I said, ah, we have to run.
So we ran away.
We were running for about two, three days without food, without clothes--
What.
Into the forest, yeah.
So, I remember like today how my whole clothing was.
I had a pair of linen pants, no shirt.
The shoes were from the camp, with the wooden soles, wooden shoes.
And that was my possession.
That was all my--
So after three days, then got-- then it started to rain, you understand.
And it was coming down, such a heavy rain.
Like a flood.
Well, and we sitting, and you know.
I had no bandages for my leg, nothing.
So what do I do?
We take a leaf and-- a green leaf, and put it down against it.
So OK.
After the third day, you know, I was very heartbroken.
I was crying.
I said, why did I have to run away?
Why couldn't I be killed together with my father.
Yeah.
But-- So, finally, we came to a state where we had no choice.
You had to survive.
So, I-- we came into a small village.
You know, like a, what's it called, a hamlet?
Or a small village, where there were people in there.
So, in the meantime, picked up another friend.
Which, this friend, he was my-- this is, it just happens, that he was my-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know?
My-- my-- he's my daughter's husband's father.
[SIGHS] So, we were three of us.
And as it got dark, we had to go in and-- to get some food.
So I told them, I said, you know what, you two guys stay here.
And I'll go into this house.
And I'll ask for food.
I'll beg for food.
So I came on to that house.
And there was an old Russian woman.
And I tell her, I say, listen.
I don't care what's happening.
You gotta give me some food.
Because I'm starving to--
She said, look.
I know all about it.
The whole town knows that the camp, your concentration camp was liquidated.
And the Germans are going around and they telling, they're warning us not to feed any Jews.
Because they know that some Jews escaped.
So-- but, she says, you go in to my barn, where I keep my cows and my horses.
And I'll bring you some food in there.
But I'll have to cook.
I'm going to cook some potatoes for you.
So, naturally, I says, OK.
But I did not go in there.
Because I was afraid that she may report us.
So we were staying outside and look in.
Sure enough, after a little while, she gave us some food.
She gave us some bread.
She gave us some milk.
And she cooked potatoes.
So she had really meant it.
And it was OK.
And this woman particular, what happened, she told us, she said, look.
You see, these people across the street, don't you ever go near there.
Because they are collaborators.
They're with the Nazis.
The people two houses away, you could go in and you could beg him for food.
And they'll help you.
And so on and so on.
There were some people who working in the camp.
Like, there were tailors, the shoemakers, and, you know, and all kind of thing.
And then they had the people who were cooks in there, who were cooking those meals, who were baking, who were cooking meals for the Germans.
These people thought that-- while they were hiding, they thought that they'll be able to survive.
Well, unfortunately, didn't happen like this.
What happened, after two days, they-- the Germans found out where they are.
And they gathered them together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And also, this friend, my dear friend Stanley [NON-ENGLISH] Safier, he was one of them.
And they told them-- they-- the Germans, they always used to trick the people.
They always used to take, you probably heard, where they told you, they gave you a piece of soap, and to go into crematorium to take a shower.
OK?
So what they did, they told them, they said, listen.
What's happening, it happened already.
Now, if you guys want to be-- people want to be alive, you have to stay with us.
Guarantee you, you'll be safe through the war.
We're going to take you to a different camp and you'll be OK.
So they told them to gather all these dead bodies.
Because the bodies were spread-- strewn all over, for miles.
Because whoever can run, whoever could run away, run away.
But you couldn't run too far, because everybody was against you.
All the Ukrainians were against you.
All the Germans are against you.
So wherever you run, whenever they had a chance to kill a Jew, it was good.
So there were bodies all over.
So they were pulling all these bodies together.
In our camp, there was a lot of burning wood.
You know, like, in here, they call it fireplaces.
Well, so we had big stacks of wood to throw them on.
So the Germans did so-- they told them to, say, put one row of bodies, one row of wood.
One row of bodies, and one row of wood.
And then go up, pile it up, higher now, all of the bodies.
And then, they put a torch to it, and everything went up in flame.
They want these people, who did all this things, the Germans told them, they say they threw a big party, a big celebration party on that night.
And they took all these people and they told them to go up to the second floor.
They lined them up.
They told them to strip their clothes-- men, women-- and line up against the wall.
Line up against the wall, and they told them, they said, listen.
The bullets are too expensive to spend for Jews.
But, we're going to shoot.
Whoever gets killed, he'll get killed.
The other one will be buried alive anyhow.
So it don't make any difference.
So they open with a machine gun.
And this friend of my-- Stanley, he was a little guy.
He was about my size, even a little smaller than me.
He was with his father.
So you know, a father try to protect his child.
So he put him against the wall, to be the first one on the wall.
Because he figures, if they're gonna shoot, they're gonna shoot from the back.
So he'll-- he'll be safe.
Because all the bodies are going to fall on him.
So, that's the-- what happened.
So, in the meantime the Germans are celebrating.
They're getting drunk.
And they're having fun.
And they torched the camp.
They didn't want to have any-- for some reason, they didn't-- they're putting the building on fire.
As the fire started, you know, the building start to collapse.
So the floor collapsed.
So this man Stanley, he fell down from the second floor, all the way down to the basement.
Without clothes, naked and everything.
He woke up because of the fire.
He got burned.
So he also, for some luck, he escaped.
He ran away also.
And we hooked up, the three of us, all together.
We were there.
Safier.
S-A-F-I-E-R.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So that was what happened.
So now you're gonna ask me what happened later.
Right?
So, in here, it's summertime. 1943.
You live-- you're not living.
You got to be hidden.
You hide.
On the summertime, so we used to hide in the fields.
We had big, high corn fields, you know.
And we used to go out in the night and pick some food, you know, from the farmers, and this.
And it was gonna-- also, I have one incident I gotta tell you.
As my father-- like I told you, he was a merchant.
He was going around and dealing with all kind of peasants and so on.
So when the camp saw when the-- saw whatever happened, that nothing is, so whatever we had, he gave away to one Ukrainian family.
And he told him, he says, look, he said, whatever happen, if you want to give it back, it's OK.
I knew the place.
I knew this.
I used to go sometime with my father to these villages.
And I knew where he gave all this stuff away.
So when I escaped from the camp, so, you know, I needed some food.
I didn't want anything.
I just wanted food to-- So we were-- we were, this group, the three of us, we were like three brothers.
We, you know, we were going, we were sticking all together and everything.
And we used to go together.
So, one day, I say, you know what?
I know my father gave to this guy, he gave him all this-- our possessions.
I says, you know, well, let me-- let me go in and ask him.
Maybe he'll give me a loaf of bread or something.
Yeah, it's a good idea.
So, I go in to this guy.
And my two friends are outside watching me.
I don't remember-- who?
My friend's names, I know.
Yeah.
This guy, I know--
Ukrainian name, all their names were [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH], or so on.
Whatever he was name.
So, anyhow, I says, OK.
Let me go in.
I go in.
And I tell him, I says, listen, you know who I am.
I am Nuchim's son.
I say, I know my father gave you whatever he had.
I say, I don't want nothing back from you.
Don't-- don't get me wrong.
I don't want nothing from you.
The only thing I'm asking you, give me, maybe you could give me a loaf of bread, or some potatoes, or whatever.
He said, yeah, yeah, yeah.
He says, you know what?
Stay and wait for me in here.
He say, I'm going to go out and get something.
Sure enough, I'm staying, waiting.
You know, the houses in there, in these-- there were, like, little huts in there, you know?
It was very-- the windows were low.
It was all made out of mud and wha-- Sure enough, I see he comes in with a pitchfork.
He say, you [NON-ENGLISH], you-- you dirty Jew.
He say, food, I'm gonna give you?
He say, you don't need no more food.
I'm gonna kill you.
I was young in that time.
I jumped out through the window.
And I run away.
We run away.
But a few days later, we say, we're gonna to take care of them guy.
Few days later, we came down in the middle of the night.
We put the house afire.
Huh?
Yeah.
Yeah.
We were all there.
We burned up half of the village of [NON-ENGLISH].
We-- throw everything, I thought that was at least one revenge.
So now, we sitting in there.
So now winter's getting there.
So we hooked up with another-- with an olderly man.
Had you gotten any more clothing in the meantime?
Got nothing.
There was no clothing.
I mean, there-- well, I think we picked up some clothing, but-- I don't know.
Because we took clothing from some people who escaped, you know?
You know, and it's another thing I must tell you.
There were certain people, even between the Jewish people, even our own people, who unfortunately, they were only looking for themself.
They gonna-- they thought that they gonna be the only survivors in-- in this world.
So, like, this one, also, Stanley [NON-ENGLISH], remember one time he was telling me, where one guy who escaped from the camp, and his name, I remember his name.
He- his name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was, like, he wasn't the nicest man.
Nobody could say anything nice about him.
So he thought-- for some reason, he-- he was lucky enough to escape from the camp.
And he took off, as he was cleaning the bodies-- like I told you, we were taking all the bodies together-- so he took in his possession whatever he could find.
He was looking.
People were hiding money, or gold, or whatever.
And clothes.
He took from everybody, he took clothes and clothes.
He took a whole sack, of couple of sacks of clothes.
And he was-- he was gonna-- he was gonna sell them.
He was gonna do whatever.
I don't know.
So my poor friend [NON-ENGLISH], he was naked.
You know, he was nude.
No clothes, nothing.
He says to him, [NON-ENGLISH], do me a favor.
Give me a pair of pants.
Give me a shirt.
Let me clothe.
No.
No.
I can't give it to you.
He said, you don't have money.
I can't give it to you.
So, OK.
He can't give it to him.
So what happened?
He fell asleep.
He helped himself.
[NON-ENGLISH] helped himself.
So, all right.
Now we up to where we sitting in-- on the summertime, we could survive.
So now, we had another man, an elderly person.
His name was Avrom [NON-ENGLISH] Parness.
He was an elderly man.
You know, he went through more than we knew.
Were still young kids.
So, we decide we gonna build a bunker for ourself in the woods.
OK.
So we made, we got it-- we got all shovels, and axes, and everything.
And we started to build, to dig.
Where did you get the tools?
Everywhere.
We used to go into the farms.
We used to steal sometime.
We had to do it.
I mean, you had to survive.
So, we start to build a bunker.
And we build a bunker.
This other friend of mine, he should rest in peace.
His name was Aaron Berger.
He was a very smart kid.
Very smart.
He was like an engineer.
And he designed that bunker.
We had, like, a little stove inside.
You know.
We used to cook in there.
We had places to sleep, like, like bunk beds, you know.
So we were, in that time already about-- another-- we-- we met another kid.
Is-- his name is [NON-ENGLISH] Gruber.
And he's-- as a matter of fact, he's still in Paris.
He became a-- I understand he is a rich man.
I never met him since after the war.
But I heard something, I couldn't get-- that somebody-- with I didn't know if he was still alive.
So he came.
So we had-- he was badly wounded.
And we had pity on him.
So we took him in.
And then we had, another woman came in with her three daughters.
We took them in.
And we build a bunker.
And then during fall time, like now, in October, we used to go out to the fields and bring in potatoes.
That's the only thing you could bring in.
Potatoes.
And some fruit.
And so on, you know.
And we had it in there.
We were there.
And-- and the-- and we stood there through the winter.
Yeah.
Yeah.
